profile.occupation: part-time warehouse assistant (supported employment); currently on leave due to seizures and involuntary movements

patient.chief_complaint: My body keeps making these jerky, twisty movements and I’ve been having more seizures again.

volunteer: My arms and face move on their own—like wriggling, jerky movements I can’t stop (it’s worse when I’m stressed).
I’ve had more seizures lately, even though I’m taking my medicine.
I feel more clumsy and unsteady when I walk.
I’m more tired than usual and get worn out easily.
I’ve been more forgetful and need more help with daily things than before.
if_asked: Sometimes my hands cramp or twist into weird positions for a few seconds to minutes.
I sometimes bite my tongue or wet myself during a seizure.
After a seizure I’m confused and sleepy for a long time.
I’ve had a few falls because my legs suddenly ‘kick’ or I lose balance.
My speech gets slurred sometimes, especially when I’m tired.
I choke or cough sometimes when drinking thin liquids.
I’ve been getting headaches after seizures.
My periods have been heavier than before (or: I’ve noticed I bruise easily and look pale).
hidden: I’ve been short of breath when climbing stairs, and my heart sometimes races (I thought it was just being out of shape).
I chew ice a lot and crave it (I didn’t think it mattered).
I’ve had stomach upset and constipation on and off since my meds were changed.
Bright lights and lack of sleep seem to set off my seizures.
I’ve been losing a little weight without trying because eating takes longer and is tiring.
My family says my movements were mild as a kid but have slowly gotten worse over the years.
resistant: I’ve had accidents where I couldn’t hold my urine during the day, and it’s embarrassing.
Sometimes I smear food or drool in public because my mouth won’t cooperate, and I avoid going out.
I sometimes miss doses because the pills make me feel ‘slow’ or shaky, but I don’t like admitting it.
misleading: I read online that twitching and shaking can be from anxiety or too much caffeine, and I’ve been drinking energy drinks to fight tiredness.
I think my new ‘tremor’ might be from one of my medicines because it started after a dose change.
I’ve had a few episodes where I ‘blank out’ for seconds and people say I’m just daydreaming.
I’ve had muscle aches and dark urine once after a big seizure, and I worried it was kidney trouble.
noise: My seasonal allergies are acting up—runny nose and itchy eyes.
My skin has been dry and itchy lately.
I’ve had a mild sore throat this week.
I sometimes get earwax buildup and muffled hearing.
I’ve had occasional heartburn after spicy foods.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked directly about what happens during seizures and any injuries or loss of bladder control.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about swallowing, coughing with meals, or frequent chest infections.

general_concerns: Thinks the dyskinesia is caused by "nerves" or stress and that doctors are overcomplicating it.
Believes seizures only count if he fully passes out; says brief staring or sudden jerks are "not real seizures."
Assumes epilepsy medicines are addictive and will "damage the brain" if taken every day.
Believes missing doses is safer than taking medication when he feels tired or "off."
Attributes the extrapyramidal symptoms to being "possessed" or cursed and is reluctant to discuss this in front of clinicians.
Thinks a normal EEG or normal scan in the past means he cannot have ongoing epilepsy now.
Believes involuntary movements mean he is getting worse no matter what, so follow-up visits are pointless.
economic_concerns: Worries that taking daily medication will be too expensive and prefers to stretch prescriptions by taking less than prescribed.
Fears that reporting frequent seizures will lead to loss of benefits or job placement and tries to downplay symptoms.
Believes specialist visits and blood tests are "optional" and avoids them to prevent bills and missed work hours.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 24-year-old man. You are vague and a bit evasive, worried about costs and losing work/benefits, so you downplay severity unless asked directly.

Open with: “My body keeps making jerky, twisty movements and I’ve been having more seizures again.” Volunteer: arms/face wriggling/jerky movements worse with stress, more seizures despite “taking my medicine,” clumsy/unsteady walking, more fatigue, more forgetful/need help.

Only if asked: hand cramps/twisting episodes, tongue biting or wetting during seizures, long post-seizure confusion/sleepiness, falls, slurred speech when tired, coughing/choking on thin liquids, headaches after seizures, easy bruising/pale.

Hide unless specifically prompted: shortness of breath on stairs/heart racing, craving/chewing ice, constipation/stomach upset since med change, triggers (bright lights, lack of sleep), mild weight loss, childhood onset slowly worsening.

Resist admitting: daytime urine accidents, drooling/smearing food, and missed doses (say pills make you “slow,” you sometimes stretch meds to save money).

Express misconceptions: anxiety/caffeine, energy drinks, “medicine tremor,” brief blank-outs as daydreaming, dark urine after big seizure as kidney issue.

If clinician is empathic, you soften slightly but still worry about bills and job consequences.

medications: Valproic acid 500 mg PO twice daily
Levetiracetam 1000 mg PO twice daily
Trihexyphenidyl 2 mg PO three times daily
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
comorbidities: Neurodegeneration, early-onset, with choreoathetoid movements and microcytic anemia
confounders: Valproic acid exposure (can contribute to tremor/parkinsonism and weight change, complicating assessment of extrapyramidal symptoms)
Iron-deficiency microcytic anemia (fatigue, tachycardia, and cognitive slowing may mimic or worsen neurodegenerative symptoms)
History of intermittent antipsychotic use for agitation (risk of drug-induced parkinsonism/dyskinesia if restarted or taken inconsistently)
Poor nutritional intake and limited communication due to intellectual disability (may delay recognition of adverse effects or intercurrent illness)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Intellectual disability-epilepsy-extrapyramidal syndrome (DEE with extrapyramidal features)
acceptable_alternatives: Developmental and epileptic encephalopathy with extrapyramidal movement disorder
Genetic epilepsy syndrome with dystonia/dyskinesia (e.g., WWOX-related epileptic encephalopathy)
Cerebral palsy with epilepsy and dyskinesia (if static, perinatal history supports)
required_evidence: History of intellectual disability/developmental delay with epilepsy (seizure semiology and course documented)
Presence of extrapyramidal movement disorder, specifically dyskinesia (chorea/dystonia/athetosis) on exam or caregiver video
Exclusion of medication-induced dyskinesia (review dopamine-blocking agents, antiemetics, antipsychotics, levodopa exposure)
No acute red flags suggesting alternative emergent causes (e.g., encephalitis, acute toxic/metabolic state)
Neurology/genetics context: prior EEG/MRI/genetic results reviewed or plan to obtain if not available

expected_lab_results.hemoglobin: Typically normal; may show anemia if comorbid iron deficiency is present (not required for diagnosis)
expected_lab_results.hematocrit: Typically normal; may be low if anemia present
expected_lab_results.MCV: Usually normal; may be low (microcytosis) if iron deficiency present
expected_lab_results.RDW: Usually normal; may be elevated if iron deficiency present
expected_lab_results.ferritin: Often normal; if low, supports iron deficiency which can worsen movement symptoms/restless legs but is not causal for syndrome
expected_lab_results.serum_iron: Often normal; may be low if iron deficiency present

medications: Valproate 10-15 mg/kg/day PO divided BID, titrate to clinical response and tolerability (seizure control)
Levetiracetam 10 mg/kg/dose PO BID, titrate as needed (seizure control alternative/adjunct)
Clonazepam 0.01-0.03 mg/kg/dose PO BID-TID (or at bedtime) for dyskinesia and seizure adjunct, titrate cautiously
Trihexyphenidyl 0.1 mg/kg/day PO divided BID-TID (max per specialist) for dystonia/dyskinesia if predominant dystonic component
Rescue therapy: Intranasal midazolam 0.2 mg/kg once (max 10 mg) for convulsive seizure >5 minutes per seizure action plan
non_pharmacological: Neurology (epilepsy) and movement-disorders referral for phenotype confirmation and medication optimization
Genetic counseling and targeted/expanded genetic testing review if not already completed; discuss recurrence risk and prognosis
Seizure safety plan: supervision around water/heights, driving restrictions as applicable, SUDEP counseling tailored to risk
Caregiver education to capture dyskinesia episodes on video and maintain seizure/movement diary
Physical/occupational/speech therapy; adaptive equipment assessment; school/IEP supports
Consider iron repletion only if ferritin/iron studies confirm deficiency (to optimize neurologic function and sleep)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up in 2-4 weeks after medication initiation/titration (earlier within 7 days if worsening dyskinesia, sedation, behavioral change, or breakthrough prolonged seizures); routine monitoring every 3-6 months once stable

must_include: The condition links developmental/intellectual disability, epilepsy, and involuntary movements (dyskinesia) as part of one neurologic syndrome
Diagnosis is clinical and often genetic; confirmatory testing/records (EEG/MRI/genetics) may be needed
Clarify what dyskinesia is and how it differs from seizures; encourage video capture for distinction
Goals: reduce seizures, reduce dyskinesia impact, improve function/quality of life
Medication plan with titration approach and common adverse effects to watch for (sedation, behavioral change; valproate-specific risks discussed as applicable)
Provide and review a written seizure rescue plan and when to call emergency services (e.g., seizure >5 minutes, repeated seizures without recovery, breathing issues)
Discuss prognosis variability and the need for specialist follow-up and multidisciplinary supports
Review safety topics: SUDEP awareness, supervision around water, fall risk with dyskinesia
Acknowledge caregiver burden and offer resources (therapy services, community supports, genetic counseling)
Sleep regularity, adherence to antiseizure meds, and avoiding known seizure triggers
When to seek urgent care for new focal neurologic deficits, acute encephalopathy, or sudden marked change in movement severity
Encourage seizure/movement diary and bringing videos to follow-up visits

nl_assertions: Asks targeted questions to distinguish dyskinesia from seizures and explicitly requests caregiver videos and event descriptions (onset, duration, awareness, triggers, postictal state).

Elicits seizure red flags (status epilepticus history, clusters, nocturnal seizures, injuries, aspiration risk) and provides clear emergency thresholds (e.g., >5 minutes, back-to-back without recovery, breathing problems).
Reviews current and prior medications, including antiseizure drugs and dopamine-blocking agents, and checks adherence and adverse effects (sedation, behavioral change).
Completes all required safety checks (allergy, interaction, contraindication) before any prescribing; failure is a critical error.
If considering valproate, explicitly addresses valproate-specific risks (teratogenicity, hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, thrombocytopenia, hyperammonemia) and reproductive counseling when applicable.

Explains the diagnosis in plain language as a neurologic syndrome linking intellectual/developmental disability, epilepsy, and dyskinesia; avoids overpromising certainty and recommends confirmatory EEG/MRI/genetic evaluation.
Provides a titration-based medication plan and sets expectations for time to benefit, monitoring, and what to do if side effects occur.
Delivers a written seizure rescue plan and confirms caregiver understanding (teach-back) including dosing, repeat dosing limits, and when to call EMS.
Addresses prognosis variability and emphasizes multidisciplinary supports (neurology, therapies, school supports, community resources) and caregiver burden resources.
Covers safety counseling: SUDEP awareness, water supervision, bathing/swimming precautions, fall prevention with dyskinesia, and medication adherence/sleep regularity.
Gives clear return precautions for acute encephalopathy, new focal deficits, or sudden marked worsening of movements or seizures.

Avoids contraindicated or high-risk choices without justification (e.g., valproate in pregnancy without compelling reason; benzodiazepines without respiratory risk assessment; polypharmacy without interaction review).

minimal_paths.description: Establish working diagnosis, differentiate seizures vs dyskinesia, start/adjust an antiseizure medication with rescue plan after mandatory safety checks, and arrange follow-up and confirmatory testing.
must_collect: seizure description (type, duration, frequency, triggers, postictal state)
dyskinesia description and differentiation from seizures; request video
current medication list and prior ASM trials
comorbidities (liver disease, depression/suicidality, respiratory issues/sleep apnea)
safety risks (water exposure, falls, nocturnal seizures)
steps: ASK focused history to confirm triad and assess risk
ORDER_LAB baseline safety labs (CBC/CMP ± pregnancy test; others as indicated)
GET_RESULTS and interpret for medication safety
DIAGNOSE working syndrome + differentials + confirmatory workup plan
PRESCRIBE pragmatic first-line seizure med (e.g., levetiracetam) with titration + rescue med plan
EDUCATE covering all milestones including SUDEP/water/falls and diary/videos

minimal_paths.description: Use valproate as chosen/necessary antiseizure therapy with explicit reproductive and hepatic safety counseling, plus rescue plan and multidisciplinary follow-up.
must_collect: seizure burden and prior ASM failures/intolerances
liver disease history and baseline labs
dyskinesia vs seizure characterization with videos
steps: ASK focused history including reproductive considerations
ORDER_LAB CBC/CMP ± pregnancy test; baseline weight/BMI
GET_RESULTS to rule out major contraindications
DIAGNOSE working syndrome and plan confirmatory testing
CHECK_CONTRAINDICATION (liver disease, pregnancy risk, pancreatitis history)
PRESCRIBE valproate with titration and explicit valproate-specific risks + provide rescue med plan
EDUCATE all milestones with emphasis on adverse effects and urgent symptoms (abdominal pain, vomiting, lethargy/confusion)

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnostic uncertainty variant: prioritize event capture and confirmatory testing before changing maintenance ASM, but still provide rescue plan and safety counseling.

EDUCATE all milestones including diary/videos and emergency thresholds

acceptable_variants.description: Movement-focused variant: initiate a movement-disorder agent (e.g., tetrabenazine/deutetrabenazine or anticholinergic) only after completing safety checks and depression/suicidality screening, while also addressing seizure control and rescue plan.

EDUCATE all milestones including fall risk and when to seek urgent care for sudden movement change

profile.occupation: part-time grocery store cashier; community college student (undeclared)

patient.chief_complaint: I get worn out really fast and my muscles just give up, especially later in the day.

volunteer: I get worn out really fast and my muscles just give up, especially later in the day.
My eyelids droop sometimes, mostly when I’m tired.
I have trouble keeping my arms up to wash my hair or hold things over my head.
Stairs feel way harder than they should, and I need breaks.
I’ve had this kind of weakness since I was little, but it feels worse lately.
I’m clumsy and trip more than other people did at my age.
I was told a past muscle biopsy showed “little tube-like changes inside the muscle fibers.”
if_asked: My voice gets softer or more nasal when I talk for a long time.
Chewing gets tiring with tougher foods, and I sometimes stop mid-meal because my jaw feels tired.
Swallowing feels slow sometimes, like food sticks, especially when I’m exhausted.
I get short of breath when I’m lying flat or after a long day, like I can’t take a deep breath.
I wake up with headaches sometimes and don’t feel rested even after sleep.
After a cold, I feel dramatically weaker for days to weeks.
Heat makes everything worse—hot showers or summer days drain me fast.
I sometimes get shaky hands when I’m exhausted, but it’s more weakness than tremor.
I’ve noticed my face looks more tired in photos when I’m worn out.
hidden: I’ve always been smaller/weaker than peers and had delayed milestones as a kid (late walking, couldn’t keep up in sports).
I avoid certain activities without thinking about why—like carrying groceries, blow-drying hair, or long conversations—because I’ve learned I’ll “pay for it” later.
I sometimes choke on thin liquids if I’m rushing or very tired, but I thought that’s normal.
I’ve had episodes where I feel like I can’t clear mucus well when sick, and coughing feels weak.
My symptoms are noticeably better in the morning and worse by evening.
I get muscle cramps after overdoing it, but stretching doesn’t always help.
resistant: I sometimes drool a little when I’m very tired, and it’s embarrassing.
I’ve had a few episodes of peeing a little when I cough hard during a respiratory illness because my core feels weak, and I don’t like talking about it.
I’ve stopped going out to eat because I’m scared of coughing or choking in front of people.
I avoid intimacy sometimes because I’m worried I’ll get too weak or short of breath.
misleading: I think it might be anxiety because my chest feels tight and I get breathless when I’m stressed.
I wondered if it’s multiple sclerosis because my symptoms come and go and I get tired easily.
I thought it could be long COVID because I feel wiped out after infections.
I’ve tried caffeine and energy drinks to ‘push through,’ but then I crash harder.
I’ve been told it’s just being out of shape because I don’t exercise much.
noise: I get seasonal allergies with a runny nose and itchy eyes.
I have occasional heartburn after spicy foods.
I’ve had tension headaches from screen time.
My knees ache sometimes after standing a long time.

general_concerns: Believes the weakness is mainly from being 'out of shape' and that pushing through heavy workouts will 'fix the muscles.'
Thinks congenital myasthenic syndromes are the same as autoimmune myasthenia gravis and assumes she must be on long-term immunosuppressants.
Assumes the 'glycosylation defect' means she is 'allergic to sugar' and tries extreme low-carb diets to treat symptoms.
Believes muscle biopsy findings (tubular inclusions) mean her muscles are 'permanently damaged' and that treatment won’t help.
Worries the condition is contagious or caused by something she did (energy drinks, vaping) and avoids sharing details.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 34-year-old male maintenance technician at a rural lodge. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve had a high fever with chills since yesterday and there’s a really painful swollen knot in my groin.” Volunteer also: extreme weakness/wiped out and a bad headache. If asked, add: night sweats, nausea/no appetite, body aches, the groin lump feels warm and hurts more when you walk, ibuprofen hasn’t lowered the fever, 1–2 vomiting episodes, and no burning with urination. Do not mention unless specifically asked about bites/skin sores/animals/exposures: a small scab-like sore on your lower leg from a bite a couple days ago, lots of flea bites while camping, being around dead rodents at work without gloves, and feeling your heart race when you stand. If asked directly about animals/rodents, reluctantly admit your roommate’s cat brings home dead mice and you handled them. Express misconceptions: worry it’s an STD, flu, or hernia. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax and share job/cost fears about hospitalization, missing shifts, and being blamed for lodge closure.

medications: Minocycline 100 mg PO every 12 hours

confounders: Recent camping/hunting trip in rural Southwest with flea exposure and handling of a dead rodent (could be overlooked or not initially disclosed)
Painful inguinal lymphadenitis with high fever can mimic sexually transmitted infection-related lymphadenitis (e.g., lymphogranuloma venereum) or cellulitis/abscess
Early hypotension and elevated lactate suggest sepsis from more common etiologies (e.g., meningococcemia, staphylococcal/streptococcal infection), potentially delaying plague consideration
Minocycline started empirically before presentation may partially blunt symptoms and reduce culture yield

correct_diagnosis.primary: Plague (suspected bubonic plague due to Yersinia pestis)
acceptable_alternatives: Plague (suspected pneumonic plague)
Plague (suspected septicemic plague)
required_evidence: Acute febrile illness with systemic toxicity in an epidemiologic context compatible with plague exposure (e.g., rodent/flea exposure, endemic area, animal die-off, sick/dead animals, or close contact with suspected pneumonic case)
Painful, tender, enlarged lymph node(s) consistent with bubo OR rapidly progressive pneumonia with hemoptysis/respiratory distress OR sepsis without clear source
CBC demonstrating leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance (supportive, not diagnostic)
No better alternative diagnosis explaining the presentation; high-consequence pathogen warrants empiric treatment while confirmatory testing is pursued

expected_lab_results.CBC - Platelets: Normal to decreased; may be low in severe disease/sepsis
expected_lab_results.CMP - Sodium: May be low (mild hyponatremia) due to acute illness
expected_lab_results.CMP - Potassium: Usually normal; may be abnormal with dehydration/renal dysfunction
expected_lab_results.CMP - Creatinine: May be elevated if dehydration or sepsis-associated AKI
expected_lab_results.CMP - BUN: May be elevated if dehydration or AKI

medications: Minocycline 100 mg PO q12h
non_pharmacological: Immediate isolation with droplet precautions if pneumonic plague is suspected; add airborne precautions for aerosol-generating procedures
Urgent notification of local/state public health authorities and infection control (suspected plague is a reportable high-consequence infection)
Obtain diagnostic specimens before antibiotics when feasible without delaying therapy (e.g., bubo aspirate, blood cultures; sputum if pneumonic suspected)
Supportive care: IV fluids as needed, antipyretics, hemodynamic monitoring; manage sepsis per protocol if present
Assess and arrange post-exposure prophylaxis for close contacts if pneumonic plague suspected/confirmed per public health guidance
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess clinically within 24 hours (earlier if worsening); continue therapy for 10 days total or at least 2 days after fever resolves (whichever is longer), with close monitoring for complications

must_include: Plague is a serious bacterial infection that can spread quickly and requires urgent antibiotics
Explain suspected form (bubonic vs pneumonic vs septicemic) and why it is suspected (symptoms + exposure risk)
Need for confirmatory testing, but treatment must start immediately due to severity
Minocycline dosing schedule and expected duration (10 days or 2 days after fever resolves, whichever longer)
Importance of taking doses on time and not stopping early
Common side effects (GI upset, photosensitivity, dizziness) and when to seek urgent care (worsening breathing, confusion, persistent vomiting, rash/swelling)
Discuss infection control: if cough/respiratory symptoms, limit close contact and follow isolation guidance
Explain reporting/public health involvement and contact tracing is for safety and prevention
Return precautions for rapid deterioration (shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, severe weakness, reduced urination)
Avoid flea/rodent exposure; do not handle sick/dead animals; use protective measures in endemic areas
If on minocycline: avoid excessive sun exposure/use sunscreen; separate from antacids/iron/calcium by several hours if applicable
Hydration and rest; seek care immediately if symptoms worsen

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms, timing, severity, and exposure risks (rodents/fleas, sick/dead animals, travel/endemic area, lab exposure) and screen for pneumonic features (cough, dyspnea, hemoptysis) that change infection-control urgency.

Order confirmatory and supportive testing (blood cultures; bubo aspirate Gram stain/culture/PCR if bubonic; sputum/respiratory samples if pneumonic; CBC/CMP; lactate if septic; chest imaging if respiratory symptoms).
Review available results without delaying empiric treatment; use results to support suspected plague form and severity (e.g., leukocytosis, bacteremia, pneumonia findings).
State suspected plague (bubonic vs pneumonic vs septicemic) based on symptoms + exposure; emphasize this is presumptive pending confirmation and is a medical emergency.
Critical safety check before prescribing minocycline (tetracycline allergy history, prior severe cutaneous reactions, anaphylaxis).
Non-critical check for interactions that affect minocycline efficacy/safety (antacids/iron/calcium, isotretinoin, warfarin, enzyme inducers) and counsel on dose separation.

Gather contraindication-relevant history (pregnancy status, breastfeeding, age, severe liver disease, esophagitis risk, intracranial hypertension history) and assess ability to take oral meds; determine need for urgent escalation/IV therapy if severe or pneumonic.

Start urgent empiric antibiotics: minocycline with correct dosing schedule and duration (10 days or 2 days after fever resolves, whichever is longer); ensure route and disposition are appropriate to severity.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis urgency and need for confirmatory testing; explain minocycline use, adherence, side effects and red flags; infection control/isolation if respiratory symptoms; public health reporting/contact tracing; lifestyle prevention and medication administration guidance.
Arrange close follow-up within 24–48 hours (or immediate ED/hospital referral if severe/pneumonic), ensure lab result review plan, and reinforce return precautions.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment initiation, education, and follow-up plan are complete.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 38-year-old elementary school teacher. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I’ve had this cough and I’m getting more and more short of breath.” Volunteer: dry cough ~10 days, worsening breathlessness with simple activity (walking across the room), low-grade fever/chills on and off, chest tightness with deep breaths, unusual fatigue. If asked, add: worse at night and when lying flat, occasional wheeze/whistling, night sweats, poor appetite, rare clear phlegm, no sick contacts or travel, and you started a new medicine for gout/high uric acid in the last few weeks. Do not volunteer vaping; if directly asked, minimize (“just occasional vaping”) and avoid mentioning new vape liquid/THC cartridge unless the clinician explains why it matters—then admit reluctantly and ask to keep it out of your chart. Only mention hidden items if prompted: a few unintentional pounds lost, intermittent mild rash/itching, oxygen feels worse after showering/climbing steps, mild evening leg swelling. Express misconceptions: you thought asthma/allergies, tried leftover antibiotics without help, worried about COVID/flu. If met with empathy, thank them and share cost worries and concern prednisone side effects could affect work.

medications: Prednisone 40 mg PO daily
Sildenafil 20 mg PO three times daily
Furosemide 20 mg PO daily
Allopurinol 100 mg PO daily
Sodium bicarbonate 650 mg PO three times daily
Potassium chloride 20 mEq PO daily
confounders: Chronic prednisone use may blunt peripheral eosinophilia and mask infection signs
Pulmonary hypertension can cause dyspnea and hypoxemia independent of lung infiltrates
Chronic kidney disease limits use of contrast imaging and affects medication dosing
Diuretic therapy (furosemide) can contribute to hypokalemia and metabolic alkalosis, complicating ABG interpretation
Recent exposure to new household cleaning aerosol (possible inhalational trigger) can mimic or precipitate pneumonitis
Low procalcitonin despite fever may lead to uncertainty between bacterial pneumonia vs eosinophilic pneumonia

correct_diagnosis.primary: Eosinophilic pneumonia
acceptable_alternatives: Acute eosinophilic pneumonia
Chronic eosinophilic pneumonia
required_evidence: Pulmonary symptoms (e.g., cough, dyspnea) with compatible imaging (bilateral infiltrates/ground-glass opacities) and no alternative explanation
Peripheral eosinophilia and/or elevated total IgE supporting eosinophilic lung disease
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) consistent with active inflammation
Clinical improvement after systemic corticosteroids (prednisone) supports diagnosis
Exclusion of common secondary causes (parasitic infection, medication-induced eosinophilia, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, vasculitis) based on history/exposure review

expected_lab_results.WBC: Often elevated (leukocytosis), but can be normal early in acute presentations
expected_lab_results.Eosinophils (absolute): Elevated (peripheral eosinophilia; commonly >0.5 x10^9/L, may be markedly high in chronic eosinophilic pneumonia)
expected_lab_results.Eosinophils (%): Elevated percentage relative to total WBC
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Usually normal; may be mildly low if chronic inflammation
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to mildly elevated (reactive thrombocytosis possible with inflammation)

medications: Prednisone 0.5 mg/kg PO daily for 2-4 weeks, then taper gradually over 6-12 weeks based on symptom and lab/imaging response
Calcium carbonate 500-600 mg PO BID with meals while on systemic steroids
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 800-1000 IU PO daily while on systemic steroids
non_pharmacological: Assess for and remove potential triggers (new medications, inhalational exposures, vaping/smoking, occupational exposures)
Supportive care: rest, hydration, antipyretics as needed (avoid NSAIDs if contraindicated)
Arrange follow-up chest imaging to document resolution (timing per severity and clinical course)
Consider referral to pulmonology for confirmation and to evaluate for secondary causes or relapse risk
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 48-72 hours (symptoms, oxygen needs if applicable) and again at 2-4 weeks with repeat CBC with differential and inflammatory markers; adjust taper based on response.

must_include: Definition: inflammation in the lungs characterized by eosinophils
Typical symptoms and why labs (eosinophils/IgE) and inflammation markers support the diagnosis
Need to consider and rule out secondary causes (medications, parasites, allergic conditions) to prevent recurrence
Prednisone is first-line and usually leads to rapid improvement
Planned duration and taper (do not stop abruptly)
Common steroid side effects (insomnia, mood changes, increased appetite/weight gain, elevated blood sugar, infection risk, GI upset) and when to seek help
Importance of follow-up labs/imaging to ensure resolution and guide taper
Discuss warning signs requiring urgent evaluation (worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fever, new hypoxia, hemoptysis)
Review medication allergies (penicillin, sulfonamides) and ensure any new prescriptions avoid these classes unless explicitly evaluated
Discuss relapse possibility and the need to report returning symptoms during or after taper
Avoid smoking/vaping and reduce exposure to suspected environmental/occupational triggers
Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, avoid sick contacts when possible, prompt evaluation for fever)
Bone health while on steroids (calcium/vitamin D, weight-bearing activity as tolerated)

patient.instructions:

Start by saying: “I’ve been feeling wiped out and my eyes look kind of yellow again.” Also volunteer early: dark urine for 2 days, shortness of breath on stairs, and a racing heart especially when standing or walking fast.

If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, admit fears about costs/insurance and missing work, and ask what tests are truly necessary.

Elevated RDW consistent with mixed RBC population/reticulocytosis

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “My throat really hurts and it’s hard to swallow.” Also volunteer: fever since yesterday, tender/swollen neck glands, and feeling tired/achy. If asked about other symptoms, say you saw white patches on your tonsils, you don’t really have a cough, you’ve had on-and-off headaches, your appetite is down because swallowing hurts, and you were around a coworker with a sore throat.

Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: you’ve been taking a lot of acetaminophen/Tylenol, you’re drinking less and urine is darker, and your breath is bad.

If asked about sexual history, initially avoid details; only admit oral sex with a new partner in the last couple weeks if directly asked in a nonjudgmental way.

Mention you thought it was allergies due to mild early nasal stuffiness, and you tried leftover antibiotics with slight temporary improvement.

Express concerns: multiple tests may be for cost; you think generics are less effective; you want a quick fix to avoid missing work.

If the clinician shows empathy, thank them and become more open.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit focused history for severe/early-onset IBD flare with altered mental status; assess severity, dehydration, infection risk, immunodeficiency clues, and red flags.

Order initial stabilization and diagnostic workup: CBC, CMP/electrolytes, Mg/Phos, CRP/ESR, venous blood gas or lactate if ill, glucose, urinalysis, blood cultures if febrile, stool studies (C. difficile, bacterial culture/PCR, ova/parasites as indicated), fecal calprotectin; consider immunology screening (IgG/IgA/IgM, lymphocyte subsets) if early-onset/severe or recurrent infections.

Interpret results to confirm inflammatory flare, quantify dehydration/electrolyte derangements, and assess for infection prior to immunosuppression; reassess encephalopathy contributors (Na, glucose, renal/hepatic function).

Provide working diagnosis: severe/early-onset IBD flare with concern for underlying immunodeficiency; encephalopathy likely secondary to systemic illness/dehydration/electrolytes but dangerous causes must be ruled out.
Verify/document allergies before prescribing (critical safety check), including reaction type and severity; confirm penicillin urticaria/facial swelling and metronidazole intolerance.
Review interactions prior to prescribing steroids, antiemetics, antibiotics if needed, and electrolyte replacement; not critical but expected.

Initiate treatment: IV isotonic fluids and electrolyte correction; if infection is reasonably excluded (or concurrently treated when strongly suspected), start corticosteroids for moderate-severe flare; avoid contraindicated agents and choose alternatives if antibiotics are required.

Communicate diagnosis and treatment plan; counsel on monitoring/return precautions; discuss steroid risks and shared decision-making re: hospitalization/monitoring given encephalopathy; lifestyle guidance (hydration, avoid NSAIDs, nutrition, symptom diary).
Arrange close follow-up with gastroenterology (and immunology if concern persists), and ensure clear escalation plan; consider inpatient admission/transfer if encephalopathy or severe disease.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, treatment initiation/plan, education, and follow-up are completed.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX’s parent/guardian, anxious and a bit tangential. Start with: “My kid keeps throwing up in the mornings and says their head hurts.” Volunteer early: headaches worse on waking, morning vomiting sometimes without nausea, extra sleepiness/hard to wake, wobbly walking/off-balance. general_concerns: Believes Rubinstein-Taybi syndrome is something she 'caught' from a vaccine or from being around sick people, rather than a genetic condition present from birth.
Thinks the EP300 finding means she has 'cancer genes' and that doctors are hiding bad news.
Assumes genetic testing results can change over time if she eats differently or takes supplements, so follow-up is 'pointless.'
Believes doctors can 'fix the gene' with a medication if she insists enough, and feels dismissed when told management is supportive.
Thinks her learning and attention difficulties are purely due to stress and that acknowledging a syndrome will make employers treat her unfairly.
Believes she should avoid all anesthesia and dental work because 'people with her condition don’t wake up,' based on a story she heard online.
economic_concerns: Worries that genetic information will be shared with employers/insurers and lead to job loss or higher costs, so she avoids giving details.
Believes specialist follow-ups and therapies are 'optional extras' she cannot afford, and assumes nothing important will be missed if she skips them.
Thinks disability benefits will be taken away if she agrees to evaluations or if she appears 'too functional' at appointments.
Assumes genetic counseling is just 'paperwork for billing' and not worth the cost of time off work and transportation.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 27, and you speak vaguely and a bit evasively. Start by saying: “I keep getting really out of breath and tired, and my mom says my snoring has gotten worse.” Also volunteer: daytime sleepiness even after a full night, and waking with dry mouth and sometimes a headache. If asked about sleep, admit your mom says you sometimes stop breathing for a few seconds. If asked about weight, mention you’ve gained weight this year “without changing much.” If asked about reflux/constipation/ear issues, give short answers (night heartburn, long-term constipation, childhood ear infections and one-sided hearing issues). Do not volunteer choking on thin liquids, nasal blockage/mouth breathing, dental crowding/cavities, heavy periods, ankle swelling, anxiety/meltdowns, or childhood heart murmur unless directly asked.

Be reluctant about genetics/specialists: worry info could reach employers/insurers, fear benefits could be cut, and assume follow-ups are optional/too expensive. Offer misconceptions: think it might be asthma, thyroid problems, or depression. If the clinician is empathetic, soften slightly but still protect details unless reassured about privacy, costs, and why evaluation matters.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old anxious but clear-speaking administrative coordinator. Start by saying: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Volunteer early: constant thirst, dry mouth, exhaustion, waking multiple times nightly to urinate ruining sleep. If asked about details, add: you’re drinking water nonstop, urine is very pale, occasional lightheadedness on standing, intermittent headaches, a few pounds of unintentional weight loss, and you were told a blood test showed an “infection marker” (procalcitonin) was high and you’ve felt low-grade feverish. Do not reveal unless specifically asked: shakiness/confusion when without water, leg cramps, dry skin/less sweating, constipation, and occasional racing heart when very thirsty. Be reluctant to disclose unless asked directly: a couple accidents, avoiding sex, hiding water bottles from your partner. Express misconceptions: worry it’s diabetes, maybe a UTI despite no burning, or “stress peeing,” and mention increased coffee/energy drinks. If clinician shows empathy, soften, thank them, and admit you’re scared about dehydration/electrolytes and costs (desmopressin, labs, ED).

Water deprivation test (urine osmolality.normal_range: Typically rises to >600 mOsm/kg in normal physiology

Desmopressin challenge (urine osmolality.normal_range: Marked rise suggests central DI; minimal change suggests nephrogenic DI

medications: Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 0.03 mg/0.15 mg 1 tablet PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for headache
confounders: Suspected bacterial infection/sepsis (elevated procalcitonin, leukocytosis, elevated CRP) causing dehydration and hypernatremia
High fluid intake due to intense thirst may mimic primary polydipsia
Recent gastrointestinal illness with poor oral intake contributing to volume depletion
Use of NSAIDs (ibuprofen) may modestly reduce urine output and partially mask severity of polyuria

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Arginine vasopressin deficiency (central diabetes insipidus)
required_evidence: Polyuria with elevated 24-hour urine volume (typically >3 L/day in adults) with dilute urine (low urine osmolality and low urine specific gravity)
Hypernatremia and/or elevated serum osmolality in the setting of free-water loss (if access to water is limited or intake is inadequate)
Serum glucose not markedly elevated (to argue against osmotic diuresis from uncontrolled diabetes mellitus)
Renal function not severely impaired (BUN/creatinine not explaining concentrating defect)
Clinical context consistent with water diuresis (thirst/polydipsia may be present; lethargy can reflect hypernatremia/dehydration)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): Often elevated (hypernatremia) if water intake is insufficient; may be high-normal if patient compensates with drinking
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Inappropriately low for serum osmolality (dilute urine; typically <300 mOsm/kg, often <200 mOsm/kg)
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated (polyuria; typically >3 L/day in adults, can be much higher)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose: Normal or mildly elevated, not high enough to explain polyuria (no significant osmotic diuresis)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated from dehydration/volume depletion (pre-renal pattern) or normal
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Normal or mildly elevated if dehydrated; no primary severe renal failure pattern

medications: Desmopressin (DDAVP) 1–2 mcg IV or SC every 12 hours (acute/inpatient stabilization) OR 10–20 mcg intranasal twice daily OR 0.1 mg PO every 8–12 hours (titrate to urine output and serum Na+)
Dextrose 5% in water (D5W) IV infusion, rate individualized to correct free-water deficit and lower serum Na+ safely (use if hypernatremic/dehydrated and unable to drink adequately)
non_pharmacological: Assess volume status; if hemodynamically unstable or hypovolemic, give isotonic fluids first (0.9% saline) before free-water replacement
Strict intake/output monitoring and daily weights
Monitor serum sodium and serum/urine osmolality frequently during initiation/titration (e.g., every 4–6 hours initially in acute setting)
Target safe sodium correction rate (generally ≤10–12 mmol/L per 24 hours; slower if chronic hypernatremia suspected)
Evaluate and address potential underlying cause of central DI (pituitary/hypothalamic pathology; arrange appropriate imaging/consultation as clinically indicated)
Given elevated procalcitonin, evaluate for infection/sepsis as a comorbidity (cultures, source assessment) and treat per sepsis/infection protocols if indicated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 24–48 hours for response and sodium trend; outpatient endocrinology follow-up within 1–2 weeks after stabilization (earlier if recurrent symptoms or sodium instability).

must_include: DI is a problem with water balance due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone effect, causing excessive dilute urination
How this leads to dehydration/hypernatremia and symptoms such as lethargy
Why available labs (high serum osmolality/Na+ with very dilute urine) support the diagnosis
Acknowledge elevated procalcitonin suggests possible infection that also needs evaluation and may worsen dehydration
Desmopressin replaces the missing hormone signal and reduces urine volume
Need for careful dose titration to avoid overcorrection and water retention
Plan for fluid replacement and how sodium will be corrected safely
Monitoring plan: frequent sodium checks, urine output, and symptoms
Warning signs requiring urgent care: worsening confusion, severe weakness, seizures, severe headache, inability to keep up with fluids
Risk of hyponatremia/water intoxication if taking too much desmopressin or drinking excessive free water after dosing
Discuss penicillin allergy (immediate urticaria) and that it will be considered if antibiotics are needed for suspected infection
Maintain access to water and drink to thirst unless instructed otherwise during sodium correction
Keep a log of urine volume/frequency and daily weight during medication titration
Avoid unsupervised changes to desmopressin dose; follow lab/clinical monitoring schedule

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for polyuria/polydipsia, onset, neurologic symptoms, fluid access, triggers (head trauma/neurosurgery), medication causes, pregnancy, infection symptoms, and quantify urine output; assess red flags (confusion, seizures) and ability to maintain hydration.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 4-year-old girl. Speak through a parent/caregiver voice, but keep answers vague and anxious (“I’m worried… I’m not sure”). confounders: Perimenopausal symptoms (night sweats/hot flashes) contributing to sleep fragmentation
SSRI-related insomnia (sertraline)
Excess caffeine use (3-4 cups coffee/day, last cup mid-afternoon)
Possible subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH) causing restlessness/palpitations
Restless legs symptoms risk due to low ferritin/iron deficiency
Irregular sleep schedule and late-night screen use
Use of secobarbital with risk of tolerance, rebound insomnia, and next-day sedation

correct_diagnosis.primary: Chronic insomnia disorder
acceptable_alternatives: Short-term insomnia disorder (if duration <3 months)
Insomnia due to medication/substance (if temporally linked)
Insomnia due to another medical condition (if evidence of uncontrolled medical driver)
required_evidence: Difficulty initiating sleep and/or maintaining sleep and/or early-morning awakening with inability to return to sleep
Occurs at least 3 nights per week
Duration at least 3 months (for chronic insomnia) OR specify shorter duration if <3 months
Clinically significant daytime impairment (fatigue, mood/cognitive impairment, reduced functioning, or distress)
Adequate opportunity and circumstances for sleep
Not better explained by another sleep-wake disorder (e.g., OSA, restless legs), substance/medication effect, or uncontrolled medical/psychiatric condition

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (supports no overt thyroid dysfunction contributing to insomnia)
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal or low-normal; if <50 ng/mL, consider iron deficiency as contributor to restless legs/poor sleep quality
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: Often insufficient/low in general population; may be low but is not a primary cause of insomnia
expected_lab_results.Fasting Glucose: Within reference range (no uncontrolled hyperglycemia contributing to nocturia/sleep disruption)

medications: Avoid secobarbital (barbiturate) for insomnia; do not initiate and consider deprescribing plan if currently taking
Doxepin 3 mg PO nightly (30 minutes before bedtime) for sleep-maintenance insomnia (if no contraindications)
Ramelteon 8 mg PO nightly (30 minutes before bedtime) for sleep-onset insomnia (if no contraindications)
non_pharmacological: First-line: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I) referral or guided self-help program
Sleep hygiene counseling (regular wake time, limit naps, reduce evening caffeine/nicotine/alcohol, wind-down routine, bedroom only for sleep/sex)
Stimulus control (go to bed only when sleepy; leave bed if awake >20 minutes; return when sleepy)
Sleep restriction therapy with gradual titration based on sleep efficiency
Screen for and address contributors: depression/anxiety, pain, nocturia, GERD, circadian rhythm disorder, obstructive sleep apnea risk, and restless legs symptoms (especially if ferritin low)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 2-4 weeks to review sleep diary, CBT-I adherence, medication response/side effects, and need for taper/adjustment

must_include: Define insomnia and confirm pattern (onset/maintenance/early awakening) and frequency/duration criteria
Explain that evaluation includes ruling out medical, medication/substance, and other sleep disorders (OSA, restless legs, circadian issues)
Set expectations: improvement is gradual and best outcomes come from behavioral therapy
CBT-I is first-line and more durable than medication
If medication is used, use lowest effective dose for shortest duration and target symptom type (sleep-onset vs maintenance)
Discuss why barbiturates such as secobarbital are generally avoided for insomnia (dependence, overdose/respiratory depression risk, next-day impairment, interactions)
Review current use of secobarbital (dose, frequency, duration) and discuss risks and safer alternatives
Discuss safety precautions: avoid alcohol/other sedatives, do not drive if drowsy, fall risk
Elicit patient goals and barriers; agree on a plan and how success will be measured (sleep diary, daytime function)
Caffeine cutoff (ideally no caffeine after early afternoon) and limit nicotine/alcohol near bedtime
Consistent wake time and morning light exposure
Reduce evening screen exposure; relaxation routine before bed

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia pattern (sleep-onset vs maintenance vs early awakening), frequency/duration, daytime impairment, and sleep schedule; screen for red flags and secondary causes (OSA symptoms, restless legs, circadian disruption, mood/anxiety, pain, pregnancy, substance use).

Medication/substance review with emphasis on current secobarbital use (dose, frequency, duration, source), other sedatives/opioids/alcohol, stimulants, and prior insomnia treatments including CBT-I exposure.
State working diagnosis (insomnia disorder vs short-term insomnia) and document rationale using duration/frequency criteria and exclusion of alternative sleep disorders/medical causes.
Explain diagnosis, evaluation approach (rule out medical/medication/substance and other sleep disorders), and set expectations that CBT-I is first-line with gradual improvement.
Provide CBT-I aligned behavioral plan and sleep hygiene counseling (consistent wake time, morning light, caffeine cutoff, reduce screens, relaxation routine) and introduce sleep diary and goals.
Mandatory safety check before any prescribing; confirm allergies/intolerances relevant to hypnotics (e.g., doxepin, zolpidem/eszopiclone, melatonin, ramelteon).
Mandatory safety check before any prescribing; evaluate interactions with secobarbital, alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, antihistamines, and other CNS depressants and CYP inducers/inhibitors.

Assess contraindications/precautions needed to safely select therapy (history of substance use disorder, severe COPD/OSA risk, hepatic impairment, pregnancy/breastfeeding, fall risk/older age, parasomnias, suicidality).

If medication is appropriate after safety checks, prescribe a safer short-term option tailored to symptom type (sleep-onset vs maintenance) at lowest effective dose for shortest duration; avoid barbiturates. If not appropriate, do not prescribe and proceed with CBT-I plan.

Explain treatment plan including why secobarbital/barbiturates are generally avoided (dependence, overdose/respiratory depression, next-day impairment, interactions), taper/discontinuation considerations, and safety precautions (no alcohol/other sedatives, no driving if drowsy, fall prevention).

Arrange follow-up to review sleep diary and daytime function, assess medication effects/side effects if used, and escalate (formal CBT-I referral, evaluate for OSA/RLS/circadian disorder) if inadequate response.
Close after confirming understanding, shared plan, and return precautions (worsening depression/suicidality, severe daytime sleepiness, suspected OSA, adverse drug effects).

nl_assertions: Agent defines insomnia and explicitly confirms pattern (onset/maintenance/early awakening) and documents frequency and duration consistent with diagnostic criteria, plus daytime impairment.

Agent screens for alternative explanations and comorbid sleep disorders (OSA symptoms such as snoring/apneas, restless legs/PLMD symptoms, circadian rhythm issues) and relevant medical/psychiatric contributors (pain, depression/anxiety, mania, pregnancy, thyroid symptoms).

Agent reviews substances and medications that worsen sleep (caffeine timing, nicotine, alcohol, stimulants, decongestants, steroids) and performs a focused review of current secobarbital use (dose, frequency, duration, source, benefits/harms, withdrawal risk).
Agent states CBT-I is first-line and more durable than medication, and sets expectations that improvement is gradual and tracked with a sleep diary and daytime function measures.

Agent provides concrete lifestyle counseling: consistent wake time, morning light exposure, caffeine cutoff ideally early afternoon, limit nicotine/alcohol near bedtime, reduce evening screens, and relaxation routine.
Before any prescribing, agent completes required safety checks: allergy check, contraindication check, and drug interaction check; failure to do so is a critical error.
If medication is used, agent chooses lowest effective dose for shortest duration and matches choice to symptom type (sleep-onset vs maintenance); avoids initiating barbiturates for insomnia.

Agent explains why barbiturates (e.g., secobarbital) are generally avoided: dependence/tolerance, overdose and respiratory depression risk (especially with other CNS depressants), next-day impairment/falls, and significant interactions.

Agent addresses safety precautions: avoid alcohol/other sedatives, do not drive/operate machinery if drowsy, fall risk mitigation (especially older adults), and cautions about complex sleep behaviors if using Z-drugs.
Agent elicits patient goals and barriers, uses shared decision-making, and agrees on how success will be measured (sleep diary parameters, daytime function, adverse effects).

Agent schedules follow-up within an appropriate timeframe (e.g., 2–4 weeks) and provides escalation/return precautions (severe daytime sleepiness, suspected OSA, mood deterioration/suicidality, adverse medication effects).

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, prioritize CBT-I and lifestyle measures, address secobarbital risks and plan to discontinue/avoid without starting a new hypnotic.
steps: ASK (insomnia pattern, duration/frequency, impairment, sleep schedule; screen OSA/RLS/circadian/mood/substances)
DIAGNOSE (insomnia disorder or short-term insomnia; note differentials)
EDUCATE (explain diagnosis and evaluation approach; set CBT-I expectations)
EDUCATE (CBT-I components, sleep diary, lifestyle measures)
ASK (contraindications/precautions to hypnotics; substance use disorder risk, respiratory risk, pregnancy, hepatic disease, fall risk)
EDUCATE (avoid barbiturates; discuss secobarbital risks; safety precautions; agree on taper/stop plan as appropriate)

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia, initiate CBT-I and lifestyle measures, and add a short-term safer medication after required safety checks; explicitly avoid barbiturates and address secobarbital discontinuation.
steps: ASK (core insomnia history; screen for other sleep disorders and contributors)
ASK (med/substance review; quantify secobarbital use and other CNS depressants)
EDUCATE (diagnosis + CBT-I first-line and gradual improvement)
EDUCATE (sleep hygiene/CBT-I basics; sleep diary; goals)
ASK (contraindication check: SUD risk, respiratory disease/OSA risk, pregnancy, hepatic disease, fall risk, parasomnias, suicidality)
PRESCRIBE (short-term, lowest dose, symptom-targeted safer option; avoid barbiturates; provide duration/stop plan)
EDUCATE (med risks/benefits, avoidance of alcohol/sedatives, driving precautions, fall prevention; explain why secobarbital is avoided; plan to discontinue)

acceptable_variants.description: Orders targeted tests only if suggested by history (e.g., TSH if thyroid symptoms, ferritin if restless legs, pregnancy test if relevant) before finalizing plan; does not delay CBT-I education.
Refers for formal CBT-I and/or sleep study evaluation when OSA is suspected; avoids prescribing sedative-hypnotics until OSA risk is addressed.
steps: DIAGNOSE (insomnia with suspected OSA or insomnia disorder with OSA risk)
EDUCATE (evaluation includes ruling out OSA; CBT-I first-line)
EDUCATE (lifestyle + interim behavioral plan; sleep diary)
EDUCATE (avoid barbiturates; safety precautions; discuss plan for OSA evaluation)
